text.content:
• Catatonia is not a discrete psychiatric condition but a phenomenon that can be seen in oth mood and psychotic disorders
  • Per DSM-V, three of the following needed to diagnose catatonia: stupor, catalepsy, waxy flexibility, mutism, negativism, posturing, mannerisms, stereotypy, agitation, grimacing, echolalia, and echopraxia
Catatonia per DSM-V
SignDescription
StuporNo psychomotor activity; the person is unresponsive and does not interact with the environment.
CatalepsyPerson maintains a posture placed by another, even against gravity.
Waxy FlexibilitySlight resistance to movement when positioned by an examiner, as if the limbs were made of wax.
MutismVery limited or complete absence of verbal response (not due to language impairment like aphasia).
NegativismResistance to instructions or external stimuli, or no response at all, without any apparent reason.
PosturingActively holding a rigid or unusual posture against gravity without external prompting.
MannerismOdd or exaggerated performance of normal actions, often appearing theatrical or bizarre.
StereotypyRepetitive, purposeless movements performed frequently (e.g., rocking, hand-flapping).
AgitationExcessive motor activity that is not caused or influenced by the surroundings.
GrimacingRepetitive or inappropriate facial expressions, such as twisted or exaggerated movements.
EcholaliaImitation or repetition of another person’s spoken words.
EchopraxiaImitation of another person’s movements or gestures.
N.B.Bush-Francis scale also includes signs of automatic obedience, autonomic abnormalities and grasp reflex as indicative of catatonia.
 

Catatonic stupor, with features of catalepsy
Public domain

SignDescription

text.content:
CategoryKey Points
DefinitionBenign, common 'stuck-on' warty or waxy skin growth seen with aging; not malignant.
Epidemiology & CausesVery common in adults (esp. > 60 yrs); cause unknown; related to aging, friction, sun exposure, and genetic mutations.
Clinical FeaturesVariable size/colour; flat or raised papules/plaques with waxy surface; can be solitary or grouped; palms/soles spared.
DiagnosisClinical appearance ('stuck-on' plaque); dermoscopy or biopsy when uncertain (needs to exclude skin cancer).
Management & PrognosisNo treatment needed unless bothersome; removal options include cryotherapy, curettage/electrocautery, laser, shave; lesions persist and may recur.

text.content:
CategoryKey Points
Definition & TypesEczematous reaction from skin contact with external substances. Main types: Irritant (direct barrier damage) and Allergic (type IV delayed hypersensitivity)
Risk & CausesMost common occupational skin disease; high risk = wet work (healthcare, cleaners, hairdressers, food handlers). Triggers: soaps, detergents, chemicals, metals, cosmetics
Clinical FeaturesItchy erythematous rash ± vesicles, swelling. Chronic disease → dryness, fissures, scaling, lichenification. Usually limited to contact site; allergic type may spread
DiagnosisClinical: rash pattern + exposure history. Patch testing confirms allergic contact dermatitis
ManagementAvoid trigger (most important), emollients/barrier care, topical corticosteroids. Severe cases → systemic steroids or immunosuppressive therapy
